message:
fix(quick-reject): force full keyword scan when an unquoted pipeline appears

Closes a related bypass to the one fixed in 5eee44c. Even with
split_command_segments() now splitting on `|`, the upstream
pack_aware_quick_reject() path could short-circuit *before* the
splitter ran when the sanitized form's keyword scan didn't see an
enabled keyword in any segment. Specifically, payloads that get piped
into a shell — `echo rm -rf / | sh` — appeared as plain `echo rm -rf
/` after redirection masking, and the quick-reject decided no enabled
pack could match.

Added contains_shell_pipeline_operator() — a quote-aware scanner that
walks the command's bytes and returns true on the first unquoted `|`
that isn't part of `||`. Recognizes:
- `|`  (pipeline)
- `|&` (Bash stderr-merged pipeline) — the function only needs to
  detect the leading `|`; the trailing `&` is handled by 5eee44c's
  segmenter.
- `||` (or-list) — explicitly NOT treated as a pipeline operator,
  so or-lists don't force the slow path.
- Quoting: single quotes suppress all interpretation; double quotes
  let `\` escape the next byte; `\` outside single quotes also
  escapes. Quoted `|` characters in user input (the test asserts
  `echo "rm -rf / | sh"` is treated as data, not pipeline) stay on
  the fast path because the quotes wrap them.

Wired into should_fallback_to_full_normalized_keyword_scan(): the
existing `<`/`>` check now ORs with contains_shell_pipeline_operator(),
forcing the AC pass to run on the full normalized command (not the
sanitized form) whenever any unquoted pipeline byte is present. The
AC pass is the only path that can see "rm" inside a payload that
will be piped into a shell.

Three new test assertions:
- echo rm -rf / | sh → quick-reject returns false (slow path runs).
- echo rm -rf / |& sh → same (stderr-merged pipeline).
- echo "rm -rf / | sh" → quick-reject returns true (the pipe is
  inside double quotes, which is data, not control flow).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
